coredata.dc:title: Labeling of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells by hexamethylene diamine modified fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Wei
Dong, Yan
Wang, Ying
Zhou, Shiqi
Ge, Xin
Sui, Lili
Wang, Jingwen
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a solvothermal method with glucose as carbon source and surface-modified with 1,6-hexamethylene diamine. In this hybrid CDs, the modification played important role for improving the fluorescent performance by introducing nitrogenous compound to passivate CD’s surface, making the CDs emit strong fluorescence. The as-prepared CDs were linked with mouse anti-human Alpha fetoprotein (AFP) antibody and goat anti-mouse immunoglobulin (IgG) to directly and indirectly label fixed human hepatocellular carcinoma cells, respectively. The cytotoxicity of these CDs were also tested using the human hepatocellular carcinoma cells. No apparent cytotoxicity was observed, which suggested the potential application of the as-prepared CDs in bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Fluorescence spectra
Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells
Cytotoxicity

Cells culture, fixation and fluorescence microscopic imaging
Optical properties of the prepared CDs
Bio-conjugation of CDs with mouse anti-human AFP and goat anti-mouse IgG
Direct labeling of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells with CDs
Indirect labeling of cells with CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption (a), emission (b) spectra and fluorescent image (inset) of the prepared CDs (0.012mol/L).
TEM image of the prepared CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of CDs (a), CDs-mouse anti-human AFP (b) and CDs-goat anti-mouse IgG (c), the concentration of CDs was 0.006mol/L.

Images of fixed cells alone (a) at 405nm excitation wavelength, fixed cells incubated with bare CDs for 2h (b) and fixed cells incubated with CDs-antibody for 2h (c) showing some nonspecific adsorption.
Images of fixed cells incubated with IgG (a), CDs-IgG (b) for 2h and fixed cells linked with anti-human AFP incubated with CDs-IgG probe for 2h (c) in dark field at 405nm excitation wavelength.

Comparison of photostabilities of FITC (a–e) and CDs probe (f–j) labeled hepatocellular carcinoma cells. The images were taken for irradiation times of (a–e and f–j) 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 24h under 488nm and 405nm excitation, respectively.
The viability of human hepatocellular carcinoma cell with CDs of different concentration for 24h and 48h.

head.ce:title: Labeling of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells by hexamethylene diamine modified fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shenyang Medical College, Shenyang 110034, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Experiment Center of Traditional Chinese Medicine Department, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang 110016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Experiment Center of Traditional Chinese Medicine Department

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ICU, Fengtian Hospital Affiliated to Shenyang Medical College, Shenyang 110018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fengtian Hospital Affiliated to Shenyang Medical College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a solvothermal method with glucose as carbon source and surface-modified with 1,6-hexamethylene diamine. The as prepared CDs possess strong luminescence, small particle sizes (Fig. A(b) and (c)) and link with goat anti-mouse immunoglobulin (IgG) to successfully label fixed human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (Fig. C). The cytotoxicity of these CDs were also tested using the human hepatocellular carcinoma cells. No apparent cytotoxicity was observed, which suggested the potential application of the as-prepared CDs in bioimaging (Fig. B).

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a solvothermal method with glucose as carbon source and surface-modified with 1,6-hexamethylene diamine. In this hybrid CDs, the modification played important role for improving the fluorescent performance by introducing nitrogenous compound to passivate CD’s surface, making the CDs emit strong fluorescence. The as-prepared CDs were linked with mouse anti-human Alpha fetoprotein (AFP) antibody and goat anti-mouse immunoglobulin (IgG) to directly and indirectly label fixed human hepatocellular carcinoma cells, respectively. The cytotoxicity of these CDs were also tested using the human hepatocellular carcinoma cells. No apparent cytotoxicity was observed, which suggested the potential application of the as-prepared CDs in bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Various nano-structures carbon materials such as buckminsterfullerene [1], carbon nanotubes [2], graphite [3], and nanodiamond [4] have been widely studied and used in the field of chemistry, physics, biology and materials sciences. Each of these nanocarbon materials shows great physical and chemical properties that will evolve in the future.

In recent years, emerging carbon dots (CDs), usually referring to carbon nanocrystals of 2–10nm in diameter, not only exhibit many excellent optical properties, such as photoluminescent (PL), photoinduced electron transfer, and electrochemiluminescence (EGL), but also present some additional advantages over the luminescent semiconductor quantum dots, such as good biocompatibility and low toxicity [5]. Therefore, CDs have attracted growing interest in biological labeling, bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic device applications [6–10]. Currently, a variety of methods have been developed to prepare fluorescent CDs, including oxidation by strong oxidant [11], electrochemical methods [12], laser ablation [13,14], microwave heating [15], etc. All these CDs prepared as stated above showed great photoluminescence. However, most of these approaches usually need tedious processes, expensive starting materials or require expensive devices, which severely limit the availability of large quantities of the CDs for practical applications. Thus, a simple and environmentally friendly method for the synthesis of CDs still remains challenging.

Usually, surface modification of CDs is necessary for their biological applications. Several agents for CDs modification have been reported, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [16–19], poly (ethylenimide)-co-poly(ethylene glycol)-co-poly(ethyl-enimide) (PPEI-EI) [20], 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) [21], polyethyleneimine (PEI) [22], and citrate and 4-aminoantipyrine [23,24]. All these CDs showed high photostability, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, which could be applied in the imaging of normal and cancer cells.

In this study, we report a facile, economic and green synthesis route towards photoluminescent CDs, resulting the quantum yield of 3.8%. The CDs were synthesized by a solvothermal method with glucose as the carbon source and modified with 1,6-hexamethylene diamine (HMDM). Then the CDs were linked with mouse anti-human AFP antibody and goat anti-mouse IgG, respectively, to directly and indirectly label human hepatocellular carcinoma cells. Experimental results showed that indirect labeling approach possess a better specificity than direct labeling and both of them are of very strong fluorescence intensity for the effective detection of hepatocellular carcinoma cells. It was also proved that the fluorescent CDs exhibited much better photostability and brighter fluorescence than FITC (fluorescein isothiocyanate, a standard fluorescence marker), showing a good application potential in the immuno-labeling of cells. The cytotoxicity of these CDs was also tested using hepatocellular carcinoma cells. It was observed that none of the cell lines show any statistically significant differences in cell survival within 48h, from no CDs up to a concentration of 140μg/mL. All these exciting results indicated that CDs-based probes are ideal fluorescent markers with excellent spectral properties, photostability and biocompatibility.

Glucose, polyethylene glycol (PEG) 400, 1,6-hexamethylene diamine (HMDM) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Goat anti-mouse IgG, FITC conjugated goat anti-mouse and mouse anti-human AFP were purchased from Beijing Biosynthesis Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was obtained from Fluka, USA. N-ethyl-N’-[3-(dimethylamino) propyl] arbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was obtained from Merck Chemicals Ltd. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Media/Ham’s F12 media (DMEM/F12) was obtained from Gibco (USA). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Shenyang Tongren Institute of chemistry, China. Water used in all experiments was prepared using a quartzose double-distilling system. All reagents were of analytical grade and not purified before using. Hepatocellular carcinoma cells (QGY-7703) used for the study were obtained from Department of Toxicology of Shenyang Medical College.

Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were acquired using a LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin–Elmer, USA). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2100 UV/Vis spectrometer (Ruili Analytical Instrument Company, China). The excitation or emission slit width was 10nm and curetters of 1cm path length were used to measure the fluorescence spectra and absorption spectra separately. All optical measurements were carried out at room temperature under ambient conditions. The size of CDs were determined by HITACHIH-7650 (Japan), for which the CDs solution were dropped onto a copper grid and dried in room temperature. During the process of the conjugation, CDs and antibody were mixed and then shaking by the CHA-S Reciprocating Oscillator (Jintan, Jiangshu, China). The hepatocellular carcinoma cells were incubated with the CDs-antibody conjugates in electro-heating constant temperature incubator (DHP-9082, Yiheng, Shanghai, China). The aggregation of CDs and its fluorescence emission in hepatocellular carcinoma cells were supported by taking photos with fluorescence microscope (TCS, Leica, Germany).

The CDs were synthesized according to a procedure described previously [21]. Briefly, 540mg of glucose was thoroughly mixed with 25mL of PEG/distilled water (v:v/1:1) into a high pressure nitrifying pot and heated at 180°C for 3h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, 450mg HMDM powder was added into the system. After that, the mixture was removed to the high pressure nitrifying pot for passivation at 120°C for 12h. Finally, the dark yellow solution of luminescent CDs was filtered with a 0.22μm filter membrane to remove the larger product, then acetone was added in the solution and the bare CDs were collected by centrifugation at 3500rpm for 15min. The precipitation was dried under nitrogen flow and dispersed in 25mL distilled water. So the CDs of 0.72mol/L (according to carbon) solution were obtained and maintained at 4°C for use [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of conjugates of CDs-antibody and CDs-IgG
ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma cells were labeled by using two kind of methods. In method 1, hepatocellular carcinoma cells were directly labeled with CDs-antibody. Typically, 100μL of mouse anti-human AFP solution (0.2mg/mL) in 100μL of PBS (10mmol/L) was mixed with 250μL CDs (0.072mol/L) solution and reacted for 1.5h at 37°C in a reciprocating oscillator after being activated by 50μL of a mixture of 500mmol/L EDC and 50mmol/L NHS for 10min at room temperature to form CDs-antibody. Then hepatocellular carcinoma cells were incubated with the CDs-antibody at 37°C for 2h in electro-heating constant temperature incubator. In method 2, cells were indirectly labeled with CDs-IgG. The human hepatocellular carcinoma cells were first incubated with mouse anti-human AFP for 2h in electro-heating constant temperature incubator. Meanwhile CDs were linked with the goat anti-mouse IgG by a similar method as described above. 100μL goat anti-mouse IgG solution (1mg/mL) in 100μL of PBS (10mmol/L) was mixed with 250μL CDs (0.072mol/L) solution and reacted for 1.5h at 37°C in a reciprocating oscillator after being activated by 50μL of a mixture of 500mmol/L of EDC and 50mmol/L of NHS for 10min at room temperature. Finally, the cells were incubated for 2h again with CDs-IgG conjugates. In addition, all the CDs-antibody conjunction were purified by means of dialysis and fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs linked AFP and CDs-IgG were recorded on a LS-55 luminescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cells culture, fixation and fluorescence microscopic imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma cells were cultured on a glass slides in DMEM/F12 media containing 10% fetal bovine serum in a culture box (heraeus BB 16UV). All cells were incubated at 37°C under an atmosphere containing 5% CO2. To label fixed hepatocellular carcinoma cells, the cells cultured on slides were gently washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4% formaldehyde solution for 10min at room temperature. For the immuno-fluorescence experiment, the slides containing fixed hepatocellular carcinoma cells were blocked with 5% skim milk powder at 37°C for 30min. Unbound CDs were removed by washing thoroughly with cool PBS. The cancer marker detections of the hepatocellular carcinoma cells were performed after direct and indirect labeling by using a Leica, TCS inverted fluorescence microscope (with a 40×/10 objective), and the fluorescence images were captured with the blue excitation.

Hepatocellular carcinoma cells were also used to study the cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDs. The cell viability assay was carried out by CCK-8, determining the absorbance at 450nm to explain the cells viability [26]. Hepatocellular carcinoma cells were seeded on a 96-well microtiter plates to a total volume of 100μL/well. Plates were maintained at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24h. CDs of different concentrations were loaded into each well, with three duplicates for each CDs concentration (the final concentration of CDs in well was 0, 2, 20, 50, 100, 140 and 200μg/mL, respectively). No CDs were added to the control cells. After 24 and 48h of incubation, 10μL of CCK-8 reagent was added to each well and incubated the mixture at 37°C for another 4h to evaluate cell viability. The optical absorbance of each well were measured at 450nm on a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and emission spectra of the as-prepared CDs emitting blue fluorescence are shown in Fig. 1 a and b, respectively, which show the desirable good fluorescence properties by the narrow peak of the emission spectrum. The most intense PL of the CDs (0.012mol/L) appeared at 451nm upon excitation at 350nm and the aqueous solution of CDs emitted strong blue luminescence when excited by a 365nm UV light (Fig. 1, inset). The typical TEM images of CDs were shown in Fig. 2 . The particle size of the water-solution CDs were about 20nm, with desirable dispersibility and uniformity. Meanwhile, because HMDM was chosen as the capped molecule, the CDs surface were abound in free amino groups, making CDs perfect candidates as biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-conjugation of CDs with mouse anti-human AFP and goat anti-mouse IgG
ce:section.ce:para: With the activation by EDC and NHS, mouse anti-human AFP and goat anti-mouse IgG were linked to CDs via the covalent binding of amino groups on CDs with carboxylic groups of antibodies. As showed in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence spectrum of CDs-antibody conjunction showed that there were not any significant shift of the maximum emission peak in comparison with free CDs at 451nm. This result indicates that coupling of antibodies did not result in any changes in the optical properties of CDs except a little reduction of fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct labeling of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the study, the direct approach for cells labeling is the utility of as-prepared CDs by direct linking with AFP antibody towards hepatocellular carcinoma cells. Compare with other routes, this kind of labeling procedure was relatively simpler. It can be seen that the shape of the hepatocellular carcinoma cells could be hardly seen at all in dark-field at 405nm excitation wavelength. (Fig. 4 a), showing that the hepatocellular carcinoma cell itself did not emit fluorescence. When cells were incubated with CDs without AFP antibody for 2h, no or little signal could be seen either (Fig. 4b). Whereas, when they were incubated with CDs-AFP conjugates for 2h, strong blue fluorescens from the cells were detected (Fig. 4c), indicating the successful labeling. Regretfully, this method presented a little of nonspecific adsorption (Fig. 4c), which is not so good for the applications in immunological labeling [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Indirect labeling of cells with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To eliminate the non-specificity in the direct labeling, CDs-IgG probes were prepared and used for the specific labeling of hepatocellular carcinoma cells. In this method, a primary antibody (mouse anti-human AFP) was firstly incubated with cells for 2h and then, after washing, CDs-IgG probes were used for labeling. When the untreated hepatocellular carcinoma cells were incubated with normal goat anti-mouse IgG or CDs-IgG conjugates, no signal was detected as shown in Fig. 5 a and b, indicating that the CDs-IgG probes exhibited very good specificity to the targets. Whereas after the cells pre-incubated with primary antibody were incubated again with CDs-IgG probes for 2h, the antigen AFP on the surface of hepatocellular carcinoma cells can be labeled successfully as shown in Fig. 5c.

Compared with conventional fluorescent dyes, CDs possess excellent photostability. To demonstrate this advantage, an experiment was designed to simultaneously monitor the changes in fluorescence intensity of fixed hepatocellular carcinoma cells labeled by CDs-IgG and FITC-IgG under 405nm and 488nm excitation for 24h, respectively. FITC was chosen for this experiment because it has been widely used for biological detections as a standard fluorescence marker. The fluorescence of FITC-IgG labeled cell faded much faster than that from CDs labeled one and almost disappeared after 2h illumination. In contrast, for CDs-IgG labeled cells, the signal could be remained stable at the end of the illumination of 24h as shown in Fig. 6 f–j.

As for bioprobes, their biocompatibility is highly important for practical applications. As far as live cell imaging applications, the effect of bioprobes on the natural physiological behaviors of cells should be emphasized [22]. Accordingly, cytotoxicity of CDs for human hepatocellular carcinoma cells was studied by CKK-8 method (Fig. 7 ). All data in Fig. 7 were average of three experiments. It can be seen that the absorbance at 450nm of the CKK-8 was almost the same for the CDs co-incubated cells and the control cells within 48h, from no CDs up to a concentration of 140μg/mL. While the viability of the 200μg/mL dose group is lower than the matched group and the difference is obvious with exposure time of both 24h and 48h. But it is worthwhile pointing out that the concentration of the CDs used in vitro evaluation is much higher than those required for potential in vivo applications such as optical imaging of living cells, and the exposure times are much longer either. Such satisfactory biocompatibility should be attributed mainly to the use of the new materials, which are chemically inert, non-toxic, and do not release any toxic species even in a harsh environment [28].

In summary, a facile, economic and green solvothermal method was developed for the synthesis of CDs. Fluorescence carbon dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of glucose and then modified using 1,6-hexamethylene for the enhancement of luminescent intensity. The as-prepared CDs were successfully used to directly and indirectly label fixed human hepatocellular carcinoma cells through linking with goat anti-mouse IgG and mouse anti-human AFP. Within 48h incubation the as-prepared CDs scarcely had detectable cytotoxicity. In addition, the concentration and incubation time needed for cell labeling and imaging in vivo are much lower than the conditions used for cytotoxicity testing. As the synthesized carbon dots are biologically compatible and show excellent optical properties, they are promising imaging agents in biomedical and imaging applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the support from the Liaoning Province Education Department (LJQ2011109), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Department (20102221) and Shenyang Technology Department (F11-262-9-21).
